1: A direct revelation: 'For behold, in those days and at that time, when I restore the fortunes of Judah and Jerusalem.' The restoration of fortunes (shuvti shevut) is the eschatological reversal formula of the prophets (Jeremiah 30:3; Ezekiel 39:25; Zephaniah 3:20). Christ's resurrection is the beginning of this restoration: Acts 3:21 (heaven must receive him until the time for restoring all things). The 'those days' of Joel point to the new covenant age inaugurated at Pentecost (cf. Acts 2:17: 'in the last days') and consummated at the parousia. Christ is both the agent and the substance of the restoration — in him the fortunes of Zion are restored (Ephesians 1:3: blessed with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places in Christ).
2: A direct revelation: 'I will gather all the nations and bring them down to the Valley of Jehoshaphat, and I will enter into judgment with them there on behalf of my people and my heritage Israel.' The universal judgment of all nations is fulfilled in Christ. Matthew 25:31–33: when the Son of Man comes in his glory ... he will sit on his glorious throne. Before him will be gathered all the nations. John 5:27: the Father has given him authority to execute judgment, because he is the Son of Man. The Valley of Jehoshaphat (meaning 'YHWH judges') is the OT's prophetic name for the eschatological judgment seat — in the NT, it is the throne of the risen and exalted Christ before whom all nations will stand.
3: A shadow: 'And for my people they have cast lots; they have traded a boy for a prostitute, and sold a girl for wine and have drunk it.' The commodification of covenant people — traded, sold, bartered — is the type of Christ's own humiliation. Matthew 27:35: they divided his garments among them by casting lots (fulfilling Psalms 22:18). Matthew 26:15: Judas betrayed him for thirty pieces of silver — Christ sold for the price of a slave (Exodus 21:32). The trading of YHWH's people for the cheapest prices (a prostitute, a cup of wine) is the type of the one who was sold and traded at the deepest humiliation — whose price then purchased not wine but the world.
4: A shadow: 'But you — what are you to me, O Tyre and Sidon, and all the regions of Philistia?' YHWH's challenge to the trading nations who had exploited his people finds its NT echo in Christ's challenge to the cities that refused to repent. Matthew 11:21–22: woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! For if the mighty works done in you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes. But I tell you, it will be more bearable on the day of judgment for Tyre and Sidon than for you. The trading cities of Joel become the resistant cities of Jesus's Galilean ministry — YHWH's call to account remains the same.
5: A shadow: 'For you have taken my silver and my gold, and have carried my rich treasures into your temples.' The plunder of YHWH's treasures — his silver and gold — by the trading nations is the type of every exploitation that will be accounted for in the final judgment. Christ's cleansing of the temple (John 2:13–17; Matthew 21:12–13) is the enacted judgment against those who turned the house of prayer into a den of robbers — the same commercialization of what is sacred that Joel condemns. The treasures belong to YHWH and are temporarily taken; the final restoration returns them to their proper owner (Revelation 21:24–26: the nations will bring their glory into the new Jerusalem).
6: A shadow: 'You have sold the people of Judah and Jerusalem to the Greeks, removing them far from their own border.' The diaspora — YHWH's people scattered to the ends of the earth by slave traders — is the type of the scattering that Christ came to reverse. John 11:52: Jesus would die to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad. Ephesians 2:13: you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ. The people removed far from their own border are brought back — not merely to a geographic land but to the Father's house (John 14:2–3).
7: A direct revelation: 'Behold, I will rouse them from the place to which you sold them, and I will return your payment on your own head.' The divine reversal of exploitation — the scattered gathered, the payment returned to the exploiter — is the justice dimension of the gospel. Luke 1:52–53: he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate; he has filled the hungry with good things, and the rich he has sent away empty (Mary's Magnificat). Christ's resurrection is the supreme rousingfrom the deepest 'place to which you sold them' — from the grave itself — and the return of the adversary's payment on his own head (Revelation 12:10: the accuser has been thrown down).
8: A shadow: 'I will sell your sons and your daughters into the hands of the people of Judah, and they will sell them to the Sabeans, to a nation far away; for the LORD has spoken.' The measure-for-measure reversal of the slave-trading nations is the eschatological justice of Christ's kingdom. Galatians 3:28 already removes the slave/free distinction within the covenant community; Revelation 18:11–14 describes the final lamentation of the traders whose merchandise is no more — including 'human souls.' The lex talionis of Joel 3:8 — return the payment on the exploiter's head — is the justice embedded in the judgment of Babylon the Great (Revelation 18:6: pay her back as she herself has paid back others).
9: A shadow: 'Proclaim this among the nations: Prepare for war! Rouse the mighty men! Let all the warriors draw near; let them come up.' The gathering of all nations for the final battle is the OT's template for Revelation 16:14 (spirits performing signs who go abroad to the kings of the whole world, to assemble them for battle on the great day of God the Almighty) and Revelation 19:19 (the beast and the kings of the earth with their armies gathered to make war against him who was sitting on the horse and against his army). Christ is the one before whom these armies are gathered — and whose word is sufficient to defeat them (Revelation 19:21).
10: A direct revelation: 'Beat your plowshares into swords and your pruning hooks into spears.' This is the deliberate inversion of the eschatological peace-oracle in Isaiah 2:4 and Micah 4:3 (they shall beat their swords into plowshares and their spears into pruning hooks). At the Day of Judgment, before the new creation's peace is established, the judgment must run its course. The sequence: Joel 3:10 (plowshares to swords) comes before Isaiah 2:4 (swords to plowshares) — judgment precedes and enables the new creation's peace. Christ's parousia in Revelation 19:11–21 is the sword that precedes the millennium and the new creation; only after the judgment do the plowshares return.
11: A shadow: 'Hasten and come, all you surrounding nations, and gather yourselves there. Bring down your warriors, O LORD.' The gathering of all nations to the valley of judgment is the eschatological assembly. Revelation 19:14: the armies of heaven, arrayed in fine linen, white and pure, were following him on white horses. The 'warriors' that YHWH is asked to 'bring down' are the heavenly armies — the angels who accompany the Son of Man at his return (Matthew 25:31: the Son of Man will come in his glory, and all the angels with him). Joel's summoning of heavenly warriors is the template for the parousia's angelic host.
12: A direct revelation: 'Let the nations be roused and come up to the Valley of Jehoshaphat; for there I will sit to judge all the surrounding nations.' The divine throne in the valley of judgment is the OT's preview of Revelation 20:11–12: I saw a great white throne and him who was seated on it ... and I saw the dead, great and small, standing before the throne. John 5:22: the Father judges no one, but has given all judgment to the Son. The YHWH who sits to judge all nations in Joel is identified in the NT as the enthroned Christ: Revelation 3:21 (the one who conquers, I will grant him to sit with me on my throne).
13: A direct revelation: 'Put in the sickle, for the harvest is ripe. Go in and tread, for the winepress is full; the vats overflow, for their wickedness is great.' Revelation 14:14–20 is a direct expansion of this verse: I looked, and behold, a white cloud, and seated on the cloud one like a son of man, with a golden crown on his head, and a sharp sickle in his hand. And another angel called out to him, 'Put in your sickle, and reap, for the hour to reap has come, for the harvest of the earth is fully ripe.' Christ is the Son of Man who wields the harvest sickle and treads the winepress of YHWH's wrath (Revelation 19:15). Joel 3:13 is the seed text of Revelation's harvest imagery.
14: A direct revelation: 'Multitudes, multitudes in the valley of decision! For the day of the LORD is near in the valley of decision.' The valley of decision is the eschatological moment of universal reckoning — the 'crisis' (Greek krisis = judgment/decision) before which every person stands. John 3:18–19: whoever does not believe is condemned already, because he has not believed in the name of the only Son of God. And this is the judgment (krisis): the light has come into the world, and people loved the darkness rather than the light. The multitudes in Joel's valley are the whole of humanity standing before the One who is himself the Light and the Judge — the valley of decision is wherever Christ is encountered.
15: A shadow: 'The sun and the moon grow dark, and the stars withdraw their shining.' The second occurrence of the cosmic-darkness sign in Joel (cf. Joel 2:10, 31) confirms its eschatological significance. Mark 13:24–25: in those days, after that tribulation, the sun will be darkened and the moon will not give its light, and the stars will be falling from heaven. The withdrawal of stellar light is the removal of the created luminaries — the guides of human navigation — in the face of the uncreated Light who is the Lamb (Revelation 21:23: the city has no need of sun or moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light, and its lamp is the Lamb). Creation's lights yield to the uncreated Light.
16: A direct revelation: 'The LORD roars from Zion and utters his voice from Jerusalem; the heavens and the earth shake. But the LORD is a refuge for his people, a stronghold for the people of Israel.' The roaring of YHWH from Zion identifies Christ as the Lion of the tribe of Judah (Revelation 5:5). The shaking of heaven and earth (Hebrews 12:26–27) from the voice of Christ is the final act that establishes the unshakeable kingdom. But the simultaneous terror and shelter — the roaring Judge who is also the refuge — is the gospel in miniature: Hebrews 12:28–29 (let us be grateful for receiving a kingdom that cannot be shaken, and thus let us offer to God acceptable worship with reverence and awe, for our God is a consuming fire).
17: A direct revelation: 'So you shall know that I am the LORD your God, who dwells in Zion, my holy mountain. And Jerusalem shall be holy, and strangers shall never again pass through it.' The dwelling of YHWH on Zion is fulfilled in Christ and culminates in the new Jerusalem. John 2:21: he was speaking about the temple of his body. Revelation 21:2–3: I saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God ... the dwelling place of God is with man. He will dwell with them. The knowledge of YHWH that Joel promises — the intimate covenantal knowledge of divine presence — is the eternal life that Christ defines: John 17:3 (this is eternal life, that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent).
18: A direct revelation: 'And in that day the mountains shall drip with sweet wine, and the hills shall flow with milk, and all the streambeds of Judah shall flow with water, and a fountain shall come forth from the house of the LORD and water the Valley of Shittim.' The new-creation abundance — wine from mountains, milk from hills, water from the temple — is the eschatological landscape of Revelation 22:1–2 (the river of the water of life, bright as crystal, flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb). The fountain from the house of YHWH is Ezekiel's temple-river (Ezekiel 47:1–12) and Christ's living water (John 7:38: out of his heart will flow rivers of living water — the Spirit). John 19:34: blood and water came out of the side of the pierced Christ — the fountain from the house of the LORD opened on Calvary.
19: A shadow: 'Egypt shall become a desolation and Edom a desolate wilderness, because of the violence done to the people of Judah, in whose land they shed innocent blood.' The judgment on Egypt and Edom — the historical enemies of Israel — is the type of the final judgment on all hostility to the covenant people. Revelation 11:18: the nations raged, but your wrath came, and the time for the dead to be judged, and for rewarding your servants the prophets and saints and those who fear your name, both small and great, and for destroying the destroyers of the earth. The blood of the innocent that cries out from Joel's land (Genesis 4:10; Revelation 6:10: how long, O Lord, holy and true, before you judge?) is answered in the final judgment.
20: A direct revelation: 'But Judah shall be inhabited forever, and Jerusalem to all generations.' The eternal habitation of Jerusalem is fulfilled in the new Jerusalem. Revelation 21:2–4: I saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God, prepared as a bride adorned for her husband ... He will dwell with them, and they will be his people ... and death shall be no more. The contrast between Egypt/Edom (desolate) and Judah/Jerusalem (inhabited forever) is the contrast between the perishing and the eternal that runs through the entire NT (John 3:16: everyone who believes should not perish but have eternal life; 2 Corinthians 4:18: the things that are seen are transient, but the things that are unseen are eternal).
21: A direct revelation: 'I will avenge their blood — the blood I have not yet avenged — for the LORD dwells in Zion.' The book's closing verse — YHWH dwelling in Zion as the guarantor of justice for his people's shed blood — is the resolution of Revelation 6:10 (the martyrs cry: how long before you will judge and avenge our blood?). Revelation 19:2: he has judged the great prostitute ... and has avenged on her the blood of his servants. The dwelling of YHWH in Zion (Joel's closing note) becomes in Revelation 21:3 the permanent dwelling of God with his people — the Immanuel promise fulfilled, not merely in a geographic city but in the eternal new creation where God himself is the temple (Revelation 21:22: its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb).
